description: Ground coffee freshness logic. Tracks how pre-ground coffee degrades over time and when grinder quality vs freshness trade-offs matter. Source: James Hoffmann's '$50 Coffee Grinder Vs Pre-ground From A $3,500 Grinder'.

inputs.days_since_ground: Number of days since coffee was ground (0 = ground just now)
inputs.grinder_quality: no_grinder | blade | false_burr | budget_burr | good_burr | cafe_commercial

degradation_timeline.description: Day-by-day taste degradation of ground coffee stored at room temperature in a sealed bag. Based on Hoffmann's cupping of EK43/EG1-ground coffee aged 0-16 days.

0.label: Fresh
0.taste: Sweet, complex, vibrant, fruity, caramels. Full aromatic intensity.
0.coaching: Ideal. Brew immediately for best results.

1.label: 1 day old
1.taste: Noticeable loss. Sweetness drops, aromatic complexity fades. Not unpleasant, but 'a bunch is missing'.
1.coaching: Still good. Minor loss that most people won't notice in a casual cup.

2.label: 2 days old
2.taste: More positive flavour lost. Negative flavour starting to creep in at the edges.
2.coaching: Acceptable. Use within the next day or two for best remaining quality.

3_to_6.label: 3-6 days old
3_to_6.taste: Progressive flattening. Sweetness largely gone. Complexity reduced to basic roast character.
3_to_6.coaching: Diminishing returns. Brew it soon — it's not getting better.

7.label: 7 days old
7.taste: 'Not a lot going on at all.' Flat, uninteresting. No longer tastes like specialty coffee despite the price tag.
7.coaching: Week-old grounds are the break-even point. Still drinkable but no longer worth a premium price.

8_to_10.label: 8-10 days old
8_to_10.taste: Unpleasant flavours creeping in. Harsher, slightly roastier. Beginning of active degradation beyond just 'missing flavour'.
8_to_10.coaching: Actively getting worse now, not just fading. New negative flavours are developing.

11_plus.label: 11+ days old
11_plus.taste: Harsh, bitter finish. Roasty in a bad way. Rancid oil notes may appear.
11_plus.coaching: Not recommended. The three degradation mechanisms have done their work: volatile loss, staling reactions, and oil oxidation.

degradation_mechanisms.description: Aromatic compounds literally float away into the atmosphere. This is irreversible — lost flavour cannot be recovered.
degradation_mechanisms.primary_effect: Loss of sweetness, complexity, and aromatic intensity

degradation_mechanisms.description: Chemical compounds combine and create new flavours that are generally unpleasant.
Fats and oils in the coffee oxidize and go rancid. Rancid oil smells slightly fishy.

grinder_quality_vs_freshness.description: At what point does fresh coffee from a cheap grinder beat aged coffee from a great grinder? Based on Hoffmann's comparative tasting and Prima Coffee's research.

blade_grinder.detail: 7-day-old pre-ground from a commercial grinder was STILL better than fresh from a blade grinder. Blade grinders produce extremely uneven particles — boulders and dust — causing simultaneous over and under-extraction.
blade_grinder.coaching: If you only have a blade grinder, buying pre-ground from a cafe with a good grinder and using it within a week may actually produce better coffee.

false_burr_grinder.detail: Similar to blade grinder results. False burr grinders look like burr grinders but perform closer to blade grinders. 7-day pre-ground still competitive.
false_burr_grinder.coaching: False burr grinders are misleading — they look professional but produce inconsistent grinds. Same advice as blade: cafe pre-ground within a week may be better.

budget_burr_grinder.detail: Fresh from a decent burr grinder (like Baratza ESP, ~$160) was better than 1-day-old pre-ground from a fancy grinder. The freshness advantage of grinding at home with a proper burr grinder is immediate and significant.
budget_burr_grinder.coaching: A decent burr grinder beats pre-ground every time. This is the minimum recommended grinder tier for home brewing.

good_burr_grinder.detail: Fresh from a good grinder is the gold standard. No pre-ground can match it.
good_burr_grinder.coaching: You have the best setup. Always grind fresh, right before brewing.

grinder_quality_vs_freshness.key_insight: Grinder quality and freshness are not independent variables. A great grinder's output degrades over time just like a cheap grinder's — it just starts from a higher point. The crossover shows where fresh-from-cheap beats aged-from-great.

practical_advice.no_grinder: Buy pre-ground from a cafe that grinds to order on a commercial grinder. Use within 5-7 days. Store sealed. This is better than buying a blade or false burr grinder.
practical_advice.blade_or_false_burr: Consider getting pre-ground from a cafe instead. Your grinder may be making your coffee worse, not better. Upgrade to a budget burr grinder when budget allows.
practical_advice.budget_burr_or_better: Always grind fresh. Your grinder is good enough that freshness gives you a clear advantage over any pre-ground option.

freezer_storage_for_grounds.description: Hoffmann tested freezing ground coffee to see if it extends the freshness window.

findings.detail: Frozen grounds showed similar degradation pattern to room-temp grounds. Freezing slows but does not stop the three degradation mechanisms. The volatile loss has already happened at the moment of grinding — you can't freeze flavour back in.
findings.practical_advice: If you must store grounds, freezing is better than room temp, but don't expect miracles. Use within 5-7 days even if frozen.
findings.comparison_to_whole_beans: Freezing WHOLE beans is far more effective than freezing grounds. Whole beans have minimal surface area exposed — grounds have massive surface area, so most volatile loss happens at the moment of grinding, before you even get them to the freezer.

pre_ground_user_detected.trigger: User indicates they use pre-ground coffee or have no grinder
immediate_questions: How old are your grounds? (When were they ground?)
Where did you buy/grind them? (Cafe grinder? Supermarket shelf? Home grinder?)

grounds_under_3_days_from_good_source.action: Proceed with diagnosis. Grounds are fresh enough that technique matters.
grounds_under_3_days_from_good_source.note: Mention that grinding fresh would improve things, but don't derail the current conversation.

grounds_3_to_7_days.action: Proceed with diagnosis but add freshness caveat.
grounds_3_to_7_days.caveat: Your grounds are {days_old} days old — some flatness or reduced sweetness is from aging, not technique. The fixes below will help, but fresher grounds would make the biggest difference.

grounds_over_7_days.action: Proceed with diagnosis. Append freshness caveat after technique fix.
grounds_over_7_days.caveat: Your grounds are {days_old} days old. Most positive flavour has faded and some negative flavours are developing. The technique fix above will still help, but fresher grounds would make the biggest difference — either grind at home or buy pre-ground from a cafe and use within a week.

supermarket_pre_ground.action: Proceed with diagnosis. Append freshness caveat after technique fix.
supermarket_pre_ground.caveat: Store-bought pre-ground coffee was likely ground weeks or months ago. The technique fix above will help you get the most out of it, but for a bigger upgrade next time, consider buying from a roaster who grinds to order, or a basic hand grinder (~$20-30).

grinder_upgrade_path.description: Recommended upgrade path based on current grinder and how much improvement each step gives.

path.message: The single biggest upgrade in coffee quality per dollar spent. Even a basic hand burr grinder produces dramatically better coffee than any pre-ground option.
Consistent particle size eliminates the simultaneous over/under-extraction that blade grinders cause. This is where coffee starts tasting like the roaster intended.
Diminishing returns but real improvement in sweetness, clarity, and consistency. Worth it if coffee is a daily ritual you want to optimize.
